title: Generators and lazy pipelines

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Generators and lazy pipelines; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Language Reference; Python 3.14; Data Model iterator protocol plus yield expressions, generator functions, and decorators; atomic topic: Generators and lazy pipelines
sources.claim: A generator function suspends at yield and resumes with preserved local state, producing a lazy one-pass iterator whose work occurs only as values are requested.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial §§4.7 and 5.1-5.6: iteration, range, comprehensions, and sequence techniques; atomic topic: Generators and lazy pipelines
sources.claim: A generator function suspends at yield and resumes with preserved local state, producing a lazy one-pass iterator whose work occurs only as values are requested.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A generator function suspends at yield and resumes with preserved local state, producing a lazy one-pass iterator whose work occurs only as values are requested.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A chunks generator yields bounded slices one at a time and is tested for empty input, exact division, and a final partial chunk. Your goal is to implement and test a lazy generator pipeline without accidental exhaustion. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Core collections and text; Functions, scope and recursion; Iterable and iterator protocols; Scope and closures. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A generator function suspends at yield and resumes with preserved local state, producing a lazy one-pass iterator whose work occurs only as values are requested. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Construction does not run the body, exceptions appear during consumption, resources need close/finally care, infinite generators need bounds, and reuse requires creating a new generator. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Calling a generator function executes it immediately and the returned generator can be iterated repeatedly from the beginning.

includes: Use yield and generator expressions for lazy stateful production with finite-consumption awareness.
Implement and test a lazy generator pipeline without accidental exhaustion.
excludes: Construction does not run the body, exceptions appear during consumption, resources need close/finally care, infinite generators need bounds, and reuse requires creating a new generator.

outcomes: Implement and test a lazy generator pipeline without accidental exhaustion.

examples.title: Generators and lazy pipelines: direct language case
examples.situation: A chunks generator yields bounded slices one at a time and is tested for empty input, exact division, and a final partial chunk.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A chunks generator yields bounded slices one at a time and is tested for empty input, exact division, and a final partial chunk.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: A generator body runs lazily during one-pass consumption and preserves suspended state; create a new generator for another traversal and close owned resources deliberately.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because A generator body runs lazily during one-pass consumption and preserves suspended state; create a new generator for another traversal and close owned resources deliberately.

examples.title: Generators and lazy pipelines: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A file-line generator owns its file inside a generator context and the consumer uses a bounded loop so cleanup is deterministic on early exit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A file-line generator owns its file inside a generator context and the consumer uses a bounded loop so cleanup is deterministic on early exit.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Construction does not run the body, exceptions appear during consumption, resources need close/finally care, infinite generators need bounds, and reuse requires creating a new generator.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Calling a generator function executes it immediately and the returned generator can be iterated repeatedly from the beginning.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and test a lazy generator pipeline without accidental exhaustion.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Calling a generator function executes it immediately and the returned generator can be iterated repeatedly from the beginning.
misconceptions.correction: A generator body runs lazily during one-pass consumption and preserves suspended state; create a new generator for another traversal and close owned resources deliberately.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Calling a generator function executes it immediately and the returned generator can be iterated repeatedly from the beginning.

analogy.example: a one-pass production route that can build a bounded result, expose an iterator, pause lazily, or wrap a callable can introduce Generators and lazy pipelines, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: StopIteration, suspension, close behavior, metadata, wrapper order, and memory allocation require exact Python semantics

Keep this boundary in view: Construction does not run the body, exceptions appear during consumption, resources need close/finally care, infinite generators need bounds, and reuse requires creating a new generator.
